intro:
The book of Esther is anonymous; its author is unknown. The writer was evidently a Jew intimately familiar with Persian customs, the royal protocol of Susa, and the reign of Ahasuerus (Xerxes), writing after the events it records to explain the origin of the feast of Purim.

Jewish tradition variously credited Mordecai (who is said to have written an account, Esther 9:20), Ezra, or the Men of the Great Synagogue. The book is famous for never once mentioning the name of God — an omission long discussed, usually understood as a deliberate device that lets God's hidden providence speak through the events themselves.

sections.title: Traditional attributions
sections.body: That Mordecai ‘wrote these things’ and sent letters establishing Purim (Esther 9:20, 9:32) led many to regard him as the author or chief source. The Talmud assigns the book to the Great Synagogue. Whoever the writer, the detailed court knowledge points to someone close to the events within the Persian Jewish community.

sections.title: Date and setting
sections.body: The narrative is set in Susa during the reign of Ahasuerus (Xerxes I, 486–465 BC). The book was composed after his reign — it speaks of his deeds as already chronicled — most likely in the later Persian period, for a Jewish readership of the dispersion.
